nativeRange.habitat: Pristine blackwater tributaries and flooded forest margins of the upper Rio Negro basin near São Gabriel da Cachoeira, Amazonas state; tea-colored water with extremely low conductivity, very soft and acidic conditions, dark sandy substrate

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (sharp gravel damages barbels)
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and cover
dense planting with open digging areas
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration

diet.notes: Benthic forager; accepts high-quality sinking pellets, wafers, and tablets. Readily takes small frozen and live foods including bloodworm, Tubifex, daphnia, and Artemia. Should not be relied upon to clean up tankmates' leftovers — feed directly with sinking foods. Soft blackwater conditions improve long-term health and feeding response.

goodWith: small South American characins (cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras, ember tetras)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Nicklas Iversen (user: Corydoras-adolfoi). 4128×2322 px, high resolution, shows two specimens clearly. Uploaded 2019-07-06.

summary: A striking blackwater Cory from the upper Rio Negro, Brazil, recognizable by its bold black saddle and orange blaze behind the head. Requires soft, acidic conditions and groups of 6+. Reclassified as Hoplisoma adolfoi (Dias et al., 2024) — the trade name Corydoras adolfoi remains in widespread use.
careNotes:
Corydoras adolfoi (currently valid name: Hoplisoma adolfoi per the 2024 Corydoradinae phylogenomic revision by Dias et al. in Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society) originates exclusively from the upper Rio Negro basin near São Gabriel da Cachoeira in Amazonas state, Brazil — one of the most ion-poor, tea-colored blackwater systems on earth. To keep this species in good long-term health, the aquarium should closely replicate these conditions: very soft water (gH 1–8, kH 0–4), acidic pH (ideally 5.5–6.5, tolerated to 7.0), low conductivity, and dim lighting. Tannins from driftwood or dried leaf litter help maintain water chemistry and reduce stress. Fine sand substrate is essential — sharp gravel erodes the delicate barbels, which are critical sensory organs for foraging, and barbel damage is a common health issue in improperly set-up tanks. A minimum group of 6 individuals is strongly recommended; this species is gregarious and will display more natural behavior and reduced stress in larger groups. Feed directly with sinking foods as this fish should not rely on picking up leftovers.

Confusingly similar species: (1) Hoplisoma duplicareum (formerly C. duplicareus) — nearly identical but has a longer, broader black dorsal-saddle bar extending further down the flank, and serrations on the posterior edge of the pectoral-fin spine (requires magnification to confirm); often sold mixed with or instead of adolfoi. (2) Brochis imitator (formerly C. imitator) — differs in snout length and fin morphology; now placed in a different genus following the 2024 revision. Careful identification at purchase is advised. Corydoradinae possess locking pectoral and dorsal spines that can deliver a mild sting and can also secrete a skin toxin under stress — handle with care and transport only with other Corydoradinae.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer typical of the genus. Condition adults with varied live and frozen foods. Spawning is triggered by large (50–70%) partial water changes with slightly cooler, very soft, acidic water combined with increased surface agitation. A ratio of 2 males per female is standard. Eggs (typically 25–30 per spawning, laid over 1–2 weeks) are deposited on glass surfaces, fine-leaved plants, or spawning mops. Remove adults after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days at 24°C. First foods: infusoria, microworms, and freshly hatched Artemia nauplii. Fry are delicate and water quality must be maintained with frequent small changes. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the strict water parameter requirements and sensitive fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras adolfoi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (55–60 mm SL), temperature (20–26°C), pH (4.0–7.0), hardness (18–90 ppm as CaCO3), native range (upper Rio Negro near São Gabriel da Cachoeira, Brazil), habitat (blackwater tributaries and flooded forest), diet (omnivore, sinking foods plus small live/frozen), breeding protocol (2 male:1 female, large cool water change trigger, 3–4 day incubation), and confusion notes with C. duplicareus.

additional.name: FishBase — Hoplisoma adolfoi
additional.notes: Confirmed currently accepted name as Hoplisoma adolfoi (Burgess, 1982); adult size 5.7 cm SL; Negro River basin distribution; temperature 22–26°C; pH 6.0–8.0; family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes; trophic level 3.0 (benthic/omnivore); IUCN Least Concern. Note FishBase pH range is broader than blackwater-specialist sources; SeriouslyFish/AquaInfo preferred for captive parameters.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Hoplisoma adolfoi
additional.notes: Confirmed confusion with C. duplicareus (broader stripe) and C. imitator (longer snout); adult size to 6 cm; temperature 22–26°C; pH 5.5–6.5; gH 5–8; minimum tank 80 cm length; minimum group 6–8; spawning 25–30 eggs over 2 weeks on glass and plants; noted locking spine toxin defense mechanism; named for Adolfo Schwartz, fish exporter from Manaus.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping UK — Corydoras Taxonomy Shake-Up
additional.notes: Confirmed 2024 Corydoradinae phylogenomic revision (Dias, Tencatt, Roxo et al., Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society): C. adolfoi → Hoplisoma adolfoi; C. duplicareus → Hoplisoma duplicareum; C. imitator → Brochis imitator. Seven genera now recognized in Corydoradinae: Corydoras, Aspidoras, Scleromystax, Gastrodermus, Osteogaster, Brochis, Hoplisoma.

additional.name: Wikimedia Commons — Corydoras adolfoi image category
additional.notes: Image source verification. High-resolution specimen photograph by Nicklas Iversen (CC BY-SA 4.0) used as imageCandidates entry.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; 2:1 male:female ratio; triggered by large cool water change plus increased oxygenation; 25–30 eggs deposited on glass/plants over 1–2 weeks; hatches in 3–5 days; fry require microworms and Artemia nauplii; strict soft-acidic water requirements make breeding moderately demanding.
